coredata.dc:title: Facile optical quantification of mercury ion concentration using graphene quantum dot coated filter paper disks
coredata.dc:creator: Yeom, Sinchul
Kihm, Kenneth D.
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Oh, Tae-Sik
coredata.dc:description: There is a high demand for facile on-site mercury ion quantification methods in water due to mercury's serious toxicity. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), fluorescent under ultra-violet (UV) light, can be easily synthesized and used as a mercury ion detection probe because the brightness of GQD's fluorescence decreases with the increase of the number of mercury ions. We developed a facile mercury (II) ion quantification method using GQD coated filter paper disks in a 3D-printed well array. Changes of GQD's fluorescent brightness dependent on mercury ion concentration were measured via optical microscopy image processing. We also observed the dependency of mercury ion detection sensitivity on the amount of GQD and type of GQDs. Our approach may be easily adapted for hand-held mobile mercury ion quantification devices in the future due to its simplicity and small sample size.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot. fluorescence. mercury (II) chloride. filter paper. optical quantification. 3-D printed well array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Facile mercury quantification using GQD coated filter paper
Quenched fluorescence intensity vs. mercury (II) chloride concentration
Sample preparation and acquisition of fluorescence images

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate Dr. Mench's group for generously allowing us to use their 3D printers and filaments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of doped graphene quantum dots. Inset: HR TEM images of N-GQD and SN-GQD. (b) Digital camera images of N-GQD under different conditions (powder, water colloid, and N-GQD coated filter paper) under ambient light vs. UV light. (c) Comparative schematic of when GQD colloid droplets are dried on glass (top) vs. on filter paper (bottom).

(a) Schematic of our facile mercury ion quantification method. The intensity,
I Q
, at the mercury (II) chloride concentration,
Q
, is defined as the normalized value,
( B /
B 0
)
× 100
. (b) Test well array (top) and press (bottom), and the array (4 × 6)'s dimensions are
31 × 46 × 5
mm. (c) When filter paper is pressed by the press. (d–e) Comparative schematics showing the distribution of GQD colloids while drying if the filter paper (d) is not pressed and (e) is pressed. (f–i) SEM images of (f and g) pristine filter paper and (h and i) 10 μL 200 ppm N-GQD coated filter paper. (j) Raman spectrum of N-GQD powder, pristine filter paper, and 10 μL 200 ppm N-GQD coated filter paper.

GQD concentration and mercury ion detection sensitivity. The concentration units are ppm. (a) Digital camera images showing mixtures of 0.5 g N-GQD colloids and 0.5 g mercury (II) chloride solutions of different concentrations under UV light. (b) Schematic explaining the difference of quenched fluorescence intensity depending on N-GQD concentrations when quenched by a 0.5 g 20 ppm HgCl2 solution. One GQD model corresponds to 5 ppm in a 0.5 g N-GQD solution, and the X mark corresponds to a 0.5 g 20 ppm HgCl2 solution. (c) Various normalized quenched fluorescence intensity changes depending on [HgCl2], and concentration of 10 μL N-GQD solutions used for filter paper coating varies from 1 ppm to 100 ppm. Error bars indicate standard errors of sample variations.

Comparisons of N-GQD (a and c) and SN-GQD (b and d) in our developed mercury quantification method. Legends indicate concentrations of 10 μL GQD solutions used in coating filter paper disks, and error bars are standard errors of sample variations. (a–b) When HgCl2 concentration ranges from 0 to 8 ppm. (c–d) When HgCl2 concentration ranges from 0 to 1.6 ppm.
Comparison of this work with previous works utilizing GQD-coated paper strips.

head.ce:title: Facile optical quantification of mercury ion concentration using graphene quantum dot coated filter paper disks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan, 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan, 32003, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Auburn University, Auburn, AL, 36849, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Auburn University, Auburn, AL, 36849, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is a high demand for facile on-site mercury ion quantification methods in water due to mercury's serious toxicity. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), fluorescent under ultra-violet (UV) light, can be easily synthesized and used as a mercury ion detection probe because the brightness of GQD's fluorescence decreases with the increase of the number of mercury ions. We developed a facile mercury (II) ion quantification method using GQD coated filter paper disks in a 3D-printed well array. Changes of GQD's fluorescent brightness dependent on mercury ion concentration were measured via optical microscopy image processing. We also observed the dependency of mercury ion detection sensitivity on the amount of GQD and type of GQDs. Our approach may be easily adapted for hand-held mobile mercury ion quantification devices in the future due to its simplicity and small sample size.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphene quantum dot. fluorescence. mercury (II) chloride. filter paper. optical quantification. 3-D printed well array

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury compounds can be classified into three different forms: elemental mercury, organic mercury, and inorganic mercury. Different forms of mercury compounds have different biological behaviors and toxicity [1]. Among the various forms, methylmercury ([CH3Hg]+) is the most common form to which people are exposed and a primary cause of damaging to the central nervous systems (CNS) of humans and animals [2]. A chemical precursor of the methylmercury is known as Hg2+ or Hg (II) ions [3], and Hg (II) is the most common oxidation state of mercury and very toxic [4].

Because of the serious toxicity of mercury, there have been demands for facile on-site methods to measure mercury ion concentration in water. There are commonly used lab-scale spectroscopies to determine mercury ion concentration of samples such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [5]. A portable mercury analyzer is commercially available based on AAS (RA-915 M, Lumex Instruments). Although these methods have a very low level of detection (0.5 ppt), they are still expensive.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nanometer-size flakes of graphene, have been spotlighted because of their easy fabrication, low cost, low toxicity and high biocompatibility, and fluorescence with high quantum yield [6–11]. GQDs have been proposed as mercury ion detection probes in previous works because GQDs’ fluorescence emission is quenched by Hg2+ ions with very high selectivity among various metal ions such as Fe2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Al3+ [12–14]. Thus, this study focuses on the detection of Hg2+ ions without other metal ions. Until now, a conventional way of quantifying mercury ion concentration in water using GQDs is mixing sample mercury ion solutions with GQD colloids and measuring their quenched brightness using fluorescence spectrometer or spectrofluorometer [12,15,16]. However, this method might not be appropriate to be applied for a portable on-site mercury detector, because handling liquid in a glass cuvette would be bulky and inconvenient.

Filter paper is one of the most ordinary porous materials, cheap, and easy to be cut and handled. We coated this common filter paper with GQD and observed the change of quenched fluorescence brightness depending on the concentration of mercury (II) chloride solutions using microscopic images under UV light. Our technique utilizes pre-prepared dried GQD coated filter paper disks, and one filter paper disk's diameter is only around 6 mm. Because our
31 × 46 × 5
mm (7.13 cm3) size sample well array can hold 24 filter paper disk samples, considering that one standard fluorescence spectrometer cuvette's dimensions are
10 × 12.5 × 45
mm (5.6 cm3), the volume per sample in this scheme is only 6% of the conventional measurement. In addition, in our approach, only 45 μL of a liquid sample is necessary for one test. Sample preparation is to simply drop a sample solution into a well array for drying. Furthermore, the measurement time is almost instant (~camera exposure time). Also, because GQD in the filter paper is dry, the GQD will have a similar shelf life as raw GQD powder. Thus, it is plausible for the method to be further developed as portable on-site devices. To sum up, compared to the conventional way using fluorescence spectrometer, our work differs in the following: volume per sample is only 6%, the necessary volume of a sample solution is only 45 μL, it is very easy to prepare samples, and the measurement time would be very short, and the total system cost would be quite low due to method's simplicity and low materials cost.

Recently, GQD coated nitrocellulose paper strips and a smartphone were used to quantify phenolic compounds [17]. Our work is distinguished in several points: we used regular cellulose filter paper not nitrocellulose which is highly flammable. We simply used a sample well array to confine a sample solution without additional wax coating step [17]. In another mercury ion detection study using paper-strips coated with N and S co-doped GQD, the authors tried only qualitative not quantitative measurements [15]. Table 1 compares our method with these two works [15,17]. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, there is no report of the optical quantification method of mercury ion concentration in water using GQD coated filter paper disks yet.

GQDs’ optical properties can be modified by doping. It is known that nitrogen or sulfur doping enhances the quantum yield of graphene quantum dots [14,15,18]. Fig. 1 a shows a schematic of a synthesis of nitrogen (N) and sulfur-nitrogen (SN) co-doped GQDs. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of N-doped GQD and SN-doped GQD show lattice fringes of GQDs, and we can observe our GQDs have a few tens of layers.

GQD's fluorescence is mainly explained by the quantum confinement effect [19]. It is a change of electron energy levels of graphene from continuous bands to discrete energy levels as the graphene becomes graphene quantum dots because GQD's electron movement is physical confined within the nanometer size of GQD flakes. Due to the discrete electron energy levels, electrons can emit photons with a characteristic wavelength (fluorescence) under a light source (UV light).

Because physical separation of GQD nano-flakes is required for the quantum confinement to be effective, dried GQD powder which is a collection of agglomerated GQD nano-flakes is not fluorescent under UV light as shown in Fig. 1b. There could be two ways to separate GQD nano-flakes for their active fluorescence. The first one is to make a GQD colloid by dispersing GQD powder into a solvent such as water or isopropyl alcohol. The second one is to confine GQD nano-flakes in a porous material such as filter paper. For the first case, when the solvent of the colloid is dried out, GQD nano-flakes agglomerate again because of the surface tension of the solvent, so they lose fluorescence again. On the other hand, for the second case, even though the solvent of the colloid is dried out, the GQDs supported on the porous material are still fluorescent because they adhere to the porous material surface. GQD distributions of the dried GQD colloid and the dried GQD coated filter paper are schematically described in Fig. 1c. On non-porous support such as a glass slide, GQD flakes are agglomerated and not evenly distributed, but on filter paper, GQDs adhere to fibers of filter paper without agglomeration.

We used such dried fluorescent GQD coated filter paper disks in quantifying mercury ion concentrations in water. It is noteworthy that even though the pristine filter paper has its own fluorescence (autofluorescence) as shown in Fig. 1b, we are able to utilize the fluorescence quenching phenomenon in detecting mercury ions because the GQD coated filter paper shows brighter fluorescence. Filter paper's autofluorescence comes from cellulose itself, a component of the filter paper [20], thus, it is difficult to be completely removed. Such autofluorescence works as background noise, and it lowers the detection sensitivity of this method. Thus, it is needed to find an optimal GQD concentration in coating filter paper disks, and this will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile mercury quantification using GQD coated filter paper
ce:section.ce:para: The pressing step in our method is the most crucial step, which makes consistent contact between filter paper disk and the bottom surface of wells. Because we coated filter paper with GQD by simple drying, the GQD flakes are not permanently adhered to the filter paper. Thus, when the GQD filter paper becomes wet, the adhered GQD flakes are separated from the filter paper, move around, and sit back while drying. However, the total amount of GQD in the filter paper does not change during the entire process because the GQDs are contained in a solid well. As shown in Fig. 2d, if the filter paper is not uniformly touched by the bottom surface, the drying liquid droplets will be concentrated where the filter paper touches, and the distribution of GQDs will be uneven after drying up. As a result, this uncontrolled uneven GQD distribution will cause unreliable brightness measurements. However, if we make uniform contact between the filter paper and the well using the press as shown in Fig. 2e, the distribution uniformity of GQDs greatly improves after drying compared to the case shown in in Fig. 2d. Consequently, the pressing step drastically increases the reliability of measured brightness.

Fig. 2f and g shows scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of a pristine filter paper disk, and Fig. 2h and i shows a 10 μL - 200 ppm N-GQD coated filter paper disk. Due to the GQD's a few nanometer-scale sizes, GQDs are not observable via SEM. As shown in Fig. 2j, we also tried to find GQD peaks from the N-GQD coated filter paper using Raman spectrometer. Pristine GQD powder shows two peaks around 1500 cm−1, but the filter paper disk coated with very high concentration (200 ppm) of N-GQD colloid does not show much different spectrum from pristine filter paper disk's. It is because the amount of coated GQD is not enough to be detected via Raman spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenched fluorescence intensity vs. mercury (II) chloride concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 d shows a schematic of the fluorescence imaging process of a sample. Our test well array was turned over for the inverted microscope, and a mercury lamp whose light spectrum includes both visible light and UV light was used as the light source. The excitation filter filters 365 nm UV light, and the UV light is reflected by the dichroic mirror towards the objective lens and shines the sample. Then, rays including both the fluorescence visible spectrum from the sample and the reflected UV spectrum go back to the objective, and the visible rays longer than 400 nm pass through the dichroic mirror. Visible rays longer than 420 nm pass through the emission filter and reach the camera. This fluorescence image is captured by the digital camera and the brightness is obtained from the image.

The quenching mechanism of GQD's fluorescence by mercury (II) ions is known as the complex formation of GQD-(Hg2+)x on the GQD surface [16]. The formed complex hinders photon emission from excited electrons by blocking the direct jump from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) [16].

We can observe quite a descent linear relationship between mercury (II) chloride concentration and the quenched intensity in Fig. 3c. Each intensity value is the average of five independent trials, and the standard errors of the sample variations range from 0.53 to 1.27. In Fig. 3a and b images, we can observe dark spots where filter paper fibers are relatively sparsely filled, and these spots result in areal intensity variations in each sample. The averaged standard errors of these areal intensity variations range from 5.86 to 8.36. The independent five trials showed quite consistent results from 0 to 10 ppm HgCl2 solution ranges even though such innate filter paper's variations exist. From this graph, we will be able to estimate the concentration of an unknown mercury ion solution.

Fluorescence intensity of a GQD colloid under UV light is proportional to its concentration. More fluorescent GQD nano-flakes means more brightness, and this is also applied to the GQD coated filter paper disks. When coating filter paper disks, if we use more amount of GQD nano-flakes, we would obtain brighter fluorescent GQD filter papers. Because filter paper has autofluorescence (background noise) as shown in Fig. 1b, brighter GQD fluorescence will make itself more distinctive from the filter paper's autofluorescence meaning that high GQD density is beneficial. However, to detect the lower concentration of HgCl2 solution, the fewer number of GQD nano-flakes is desirable. It is because a greater number of GQD nano-flakes requires a greater number of mercury ions to observe the same degree of change of the fluorescence quenching effect. Fig. 4 a shows the difference in mercury detection sensitivity depending on the concentration of N-GQD colloid. For the 10 ppm N-GQD colloids, we can observe the brightness decreases roughly to a half when mixed with a 20 ppm HgCl2 solution, and it loses its most of fluorescence with a 100 ppm HgCl2 solution. However, for the 1000 ppm colloids, the brightness looks not changing at all even mixed with a 100 ppm HgCl2 solution. Fig. 4b is a schematic explaining the effect of N-GQD concentration on mercury detection sensitivity. If we assume one GQD model corresponds to 5 ppm GQD nano-flakes in a 0.5 g GQD colloid, the 10 ppm colloid will have 2 GQD models, and the 1000 ppm colloid will have 200 GQD models. If the fluorescence brightness of the colloid is proportional only to the number of GQD nano-flakes, the initial intensity (
I 0
) ratio of the 10 vs. 1000 ppm colloids would be 2:200. Also, if we assume that a 0.5 g 20 ppm HgCl2 solution quenches exactly one GQD model (5 ppm), fluorescence intensity change ratios (
I 0
: I )
of 10 and 1000 ppm colloids would be 2:1 and 200:199, respectively. In other words, for the 10 ppm GQD, a 20 ppm HgCl2 solution quenches 50% of the initial fluorescence, but for the 1000 ppm GQD, only 0.5% of the initial intensity decreases. Therefore, regarding detection sensitivity, less amount of GQD is better in this case.

Although Fig. 4a and b are about GQD colloids, we expect the same tendency would be found in our filter paper-based strategy. Fig. 4c shows this relation between sensitivity and GQD amount in filter paper disks, and the tests were done following the procedure shown in Fig. 2a. The GQD amount was controlled by changing the concentration of a colloid while keeping the volume of the colloid as same as in the schematic, 10 μL. The graphs in Fig. 3c show intensities normalized by their
I
Q = 0
, when the mercury concentration of a solution is 0, i.e., only 45 μL of D.I. water was used for the test.

We determined the sensitivity of GQD in Fig. 4c as the degree of reduced intensity. In other words, more drastic intensity change means higher sensitivity to the mercury solution. For the 1 ppm GQD case, where the minimum amount of GQD was used, if there is no filter paper's autofluorescence, the 1 ppm would be the most sensitive case. However, the actual 1 ppm case shows the least sensitivity among all the GQD concentrations. It is because the brightness of GQD coated filter paper is only slightly greater than pristine filter paper's due to little GQD amount. Thus, the quenched brightness is not much different from the initial brightness
I 0
. In other words, if we lower the GQD amount too much, then we lose sensitivity because of the filter paper's autofluorescence.

By increasing the GQD concentration to 5 ppm, we observe the graph goes further down compared to the 1 ppm case. However, a further increase of GQD concentration to 25 and 100 ppm yielded less sensitive results. Thus, 5 ppm N-GQD gives the best sensitivity here. For the 25 and 100 ppm GQD, in 0–2 ppm of [HgCl2] range, the brightness strays around 1 rather than decreasing steadily, and it is because that the amount of GQD is too much to show discernible brightness changes by 45 μL of 0–2 ppm HgCl2 solutions. To sum up, as we increase the GQD concentration from 1 ppm to 100 ppm, we observe the sensitivity is maximized at 5 ppm, but the further increase of GQD decrease the mercury detection sensitivity as we examined GQD colloid samples in Fig. 4a.

One of the advantages of GQD is its properties can be easily tuned by doping. It is known that nitrogen and sulfur co-doping enhances not only its quantum yield but also mercury detection sensitivity, and it is previously reported that SN-GQD colloid is more sensitive than N-GQD colloid in detecting mercury ions in water [16]. It is because SN-GQD has C-SO x -C (x = 2,3, and 4) sulfone bridges, and the bridges allow more mercury ions to adhere to the SN-GQD surface [16]. It means the SN-GQD forms mercury ion complexes more easily than N-GQD.

We compared how N-GQD and SN-GQD behave differently in our developed method. Fig. 5 shows normalized intensity changes (
I Q
/
I
Q = 0
) of N-GQD and SN-GQD coated filter paper disks when different concentrations of GQD colloids were used. As determined in the previous sections, higher sensitivity means more rapid brightness change when tested with the same mercury ion solution.

Fig. 5a and b shows normalized quenched fluorescence intensities when [HgCl2] ranges from 0 to 8 ppm. Overall N-GQD graph shows more quenched amount compared to SN-GQD's except 0–0.5 ppm range. However, the 100 ppm SN-GQD is not an optimal (most sensitive) concentration yet, because as shown in Fig. 5d, the graphs keep going down (the sensitivity increases) as SN-GQD concentration increases until 150 ppm. Even though 100 ppm SN-GQD is not the most sensitive case, in 0–0.5 ppm [HgCl2] range, SN-GQD looks slightly more sensitive than N-GQD.

Fig. 5c and d shows test results under the lower [HgCl2] range, 0–1.6 ppm. The N-GQD vs. SN-GQD comparisons were made for the cases where the similar initial brightness,
B 0
was confirmed. The same color of the symbols and lines presents one pair comparison. The reason for about 15 times higher concentration of SN-GQD colloid over N-GQD colloid is the low dispersibility of our SN-GQD powder in water. The SN-GQD powder was not fully dispersed as the N-GQD powder did even when we sonicated the SN-GQD colloid more than an hour. Thus, presented SN-GQD colloids' concentrations are nominal values simply based on weights of mixed SN-GQD powder and water. The actual concentrations of SN-GQD colloids would be lower than the nominal values. However, because all the SN-GQD colloids were diluted from one 200 ppm SN-GQD colloid, concentration ratios between colloids are accurate though we do not know the actual concentrations of SN-GQD colloids. Because we could not identify the exact concentration of SN-GQD, this comparison shows only a rough difference between N-GQD and SN-GQD coated filter paper disk with similar initial brightness,
B 0
. For N-GQD, all three graphs do not show any distinctive relationships between [HgCl2] and the intensity, even for the 3 ppm case which is near the N-GQD's optimal concentration, 5 ppm. Normalized intensities stray around 1 meaning 0–1.6 ppm of HgCl2 solutions are difficult to be detected. However, for the SN-GQD, the graphs show a relatively stable and steady decrease in the intensity with the increase in [HgCl2] from 0.4 ppm. Also, the graphs show the effect of SN-GQD concentration on mercury detection sensitivity, i.e., as SN-GQD concentration increases, the overall brightness decreases more. Thus, SN-GQD may be used to detect 0.4–1.6 ppm of HgCl2 solutions in our method, but N-GQD is not appropriate for the range.

The sensitivity of our approach is mainly limited by the autofluorescence brightness of the pristine filter paper. In the future, if we could reduce the autofluorescence or find another porous material with the least autofluorescence, the sensitivity of this method would be improved.

N-doped GQD powder was synthesized via previously reported infrared (IR) assisted pyrolysis method [16], and the schematic of the synthesis is shown in Fig. 1a. Briefly, citric acid and urea in 2:1 weight ratio were blended by a 3-D mixer using Zr balls, and the mixture was heated in IR furnace at 260 °C for 10 min. After cooled down, the N-GQD was scraped and sieved. For the SN-GQD synthesis, ammonia sulfate was added to the N-GQD's precursors, and the weight ratio of citric acid, urea, and the ammonia sulfate was 1:1:1. The following steps were the same as N-GQD's. GQD colloids were prepared mostly by two-step dilution. For example, for a 5 ppm GQD colloid, first, a 200 ppm of GQD colloid was prepared by dispersing 4 mg of GQD in 19.996 g of DI water and sonicating it for 30 min. Next, 0.4 g of 200 ppm colloid was mixed with 15.6 g of DI water to be diluted as 5 ppm colloid.

Mercury chloride solutions were prepared by multi-step dilution. First, a 2000 ppm solution was prepared by dissolving 40 mg of mercury (II) chloride powder (Fisher scientific, 99.5%) in 19.96 g of DI water. The 2000 ppm solution (1 ppm = 1 mg/kg) was diluted to 500 ppm, and then, the 500 ppm was diluted to 50 ppm. 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 ppm solutions were prepared using the same 50 ppm solution. Sub ppm solutions were diluted from the 10 ppm solution. All solutions were weighted on Ohaus PA 84 analytical balance whose minimum readability is 0.1 mg.

Test well array (4 × 6) and press were designed using OpenSCAD software and 3D-printed. The diameter of each well is determined as 5.8 mm which is slightly smaller than filter paper disk's (1/4” = 6.35 mm) to be tightly fitted when the paper disk is pressed. The depth of the well is determined as 4.5 mm to be able to hold 45 μL of liquid while minimizing 3D-printing time. Raise3D™ Pro 2 Plus was used to print the test well and the press using Raise3D™ Premium PLA filament. Standard-setting with 100% infill density was used for printing, and other settings were adjusted to remove any gaps on the bottom surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and acquisition of fluorescence images
ce:section.ce:para:
Whatman qualitative filter paper (1005–090, 9 cm dia., and 2.5 μm pore size) was cut into 1/4″ dia. disks using a paper punch. The disks were placed in the 3D printed 5.8 mm dia. wells and pressed by the 3D-printed 5.6 mm dia. press to ensure uniform contact with the bottom surface of the wells. To coat the filter paper disks, a 10 μL of GQD colloid was pipetted onto each seated filter paper disk in the well, and the disks were dried out in an oven at 50 °C for an hour. Then, their fluorescence microscopy images were taken to obtain before-brightness (
B 0
). To measure quenched after-brightness (
B
), 45 μL of a
Q
concentration of mercury (II) chloride solution was pipetted onto the prepared GQD-coated disk placed in the test well, the disk was completely dried in the oven at 50 °C, and again fluorescence microscopic images were taken to obtain the quenched after-brightness (
B
). Each sample's intensity,
I Q
, at the mercury (II) chloride concentration
Q
, was calculated as
( B /
B 0
)
× 100
.

The brightness of each sample was calculated from each microscopic image taken via an inverted microscope, Olympus IX71. OSRAM HBO 100W/2 mercury lamp was used as a light source, and Olympus U-MNU2 filter cube whose excitation filter's wavelength range is 360–370 nm was used. The images were captured via a 4× microscope objective by OMax A35180U3 digital camera under 365 nm UV light with fixed exposure time. The exposure time was determined to maximize average brightness while keeping each color channel (red (R), green (G), and blue (B)) pixel value below the maximum pixel value (255) to avoid saturation. All images' resolution is 4912 by 3684 pixels, and the center circular area with a radius of 1500 pixels was chosen and averaged. The averages of pixel's R, G, and B values were combined and converted to an averaged greyscale value using a formula, 0.299R + 0.587G +0.114B, defined in ITU Recommendation BT. 601–7 [21], and the converted greyscale value is used as a brightness value. The calculation process was automated by a python script (Supporting Information).

Sinchul Yeom: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Kenneth D. Kihm: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Chien-Te Hsieh: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Tae-Sik Oh: Writing - review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate Dr. Mench's group for generously allowing us to use their 3D printers and filaments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2020.124168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercury toxicity and treatment: a review of the literature
J. Environ. Publ. Health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.A. Bernhoft, Mercury toxicity and treatment: a review of the literature, Journal of environmental and public health, 2012 (2012) 460508-460508.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxic effects of mercury on the cardiovascular and central nervous systems
J. Biomed. Biotechnol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Fernandes Azevedo, L. Barros Furieri, F.M. Pecanha, G.A. Wiggers, P. Frizera Vassallo, M. Ronacher Simoes, J. Fiorim, P. Rossi de Batista, M. Fioresi, L. Rossoni, I. Stefanon, M.J. Alonso, M. Salaices, D. Valentim Vassallo, Toxic effects of mercury on the cardiovascular and central nervous systems, Journal of biomedicine & biotechnology, 2012 (2012) 949048-949048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive detection of mercury with a novel one-step signal amplified lateral flow strip based on gold nanoparticle-labeled ssDNA recognition and enhancement probes
